source: VS Partners / Financial Times Global Seattle Symposium — Global technology investment: hype, hope, reality
summary: AI is the dominant structural force in technology — scale of training data is the only thing that matters
summary_extended: Baker argues that AI scaling laws represent the single most important structural force in technology for the next 20 years, with a specific quantitative claim: every order of magnitude increase in training data doubles AI model quality. This creates a self-reinforcing flywheel where companies with the largest proprietary datasets compound their AI advantages over time, making data moats the defining competitive barrier of the era. The investment implication is that large incumbent internet platforms — which have accumulated the most user-generated data — are structurally advantaged and will continue to dominate, with the strong getting stronger. Baker frames this as the key explanatory variable behind mega-cap internet dominance in both the US and China, suggesting investors should favor data-rich platform incumbents over challengers.
detail: Baker articulates a scaling law: for every order of magnitude increase in training data, AI quality doubles. This creates a powerful flywheel for large internet companies with the most data. The strong get stronger. This dynamic is responsible for the dominance of mega-cap internet companies in both the US and China.
confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'the most profound, most important thing to understand' about AI and the world for the next 20 years. Repeated and elaborated at length.
quote_fragment: the only thing that matters in ai is scale

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is the archetypal data-rich internet platform Baker is describing — dominant search, YouTube, Maps, and Android generate unmatched proprietary training data, making it a clear implied beneficiary of the scaling law thesis.
